# Test: 2 electrons in 4 orbitals

n_elec = 2

# Simple integrals

exercises.solution:
import numpy as np
from itertools import combinations

def get_slater_determinants(n_orbitals, n_electrons):
    """Generate all Slater determinants."""
    return list(combinations(range(n_orbitals), n_electrons))

def count_differences(det1, det2):
    """Count how many orbitals differ between determinants."""
    set1, set2 = set(det1), set(det2)
    diff1 = set1 - set2  # In det1 but not det2
    diff2 = set2 - set1  # In det2 but not det1
    return list(diff1), list(diff2)

def compute_H_element(det1, det2, h1, h2):
    """Compute ⟨det1|H|det2⟩ using Slater-Condon rules."""
    diff1, diff2 = count_differences(det1, det2)
    n_diff = len(diff1)
    
    if n_diff > 2:
        return 0.0
    
    if n_diff == 0:
        # Diagonal element
        result = sum(h1[i, i] for i in det1)
        for i, j in combinations(det1, 2):
            result += h2[i, i, j, j] - h2[i, j, j, i]
        return result
    
    elif n_diff == 1:
        # Single excitation
        i = diff1[0]  # Orbital in det1 only
        a = diff2[0]  # Orbital in det2 only
        result = h1[i, a]
        for j in det1:
            if j != i:
                result += h2[i, a, j, j] - h2[i, j, j, a]
        # Sign from reordering
        sign = compute_sign(det1, det2, [i], [a])
        return sign * result
    
    else:  # n_diff == 2
        # Double excitation
        i, j = sorted(diff1)
        a, b = sorted(diff2)
        result = h2[i, a, j, b] - h2[i, b, j, a]
        sign = compute_sign(det1, det2, [i, j], [a, b])
        return sign * result

def compute_sign(det1, det2, removed, added):
    """Compute sign from orbital reordering (simplified)."""
    # This is a simplified version
    return 1.0

def build_hamiltonian_matrix(n_orbitals, n_electrons, h1, h2):
    """Build full Hamiltonian matrix."""
    dets = get_slater_determinants(n_orbitals, n_electrons)
    n_dets = len(dets)
    H = np.zeros((n_dets, n_dets))
    
    for i, det1 in enumerate(dets):
        for j, det2 in enumerate(dets):
            H[i, j] = compute_H_element(det1, det2, h1, h2)
    
    return H

# Test
n_orb = 4
n_elec = 2

h1 = np.diag([-1.0, -0.5, 0.5, 1.0])
h2 = np.zeros((n_orb, n_orb, n_orb, n_orb))
h2[0,0,1,1] = 0.5
h2[1,1,0,0] = 0.5

print("Determinants:", get_slater_determinants(n_orb, n_elec))
H = build_hamiltonian_matrix(n_orb, n_elec, h1, h2)
print("\nHamiltonian matrix:")
print(H)
hints: Slater-Condon rules depend on number of orbital differences
Diagonal elements sum one-electron + two-electron terms

exercises.title: Integral Transformation

M = 4  # AO basis size
N = 3  # MO basis size

# Random symmetric h matrix

h_ao = (h_ao + h_ao.T) / 2

# Random orthogonal C (simplified)
C = np.linalg.qr(np.random.randn(M, N))[0]

h_mo = transform_one_electron(h_ao, C)

exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def transform_one_electron(h_ao, C):
    """Transform one-electron integrals to MO basis."""
    return C.T @ h_ao @ C

def transform_two_electron(g_ao, C):
    """Transform two-electron integrals to MO basis."""
    M = g_ao.shape[0]
    N = C.shape[1]
    
    # Transform one index at a time
    # (μν|λσ) → (pν|λσ) → (pq|λσ) → (pq|rσ) → (pq|rs)
    
    # First half-transformation: first two indices
    g_half = np.zeros((N, N, M, M))
    for p in range(N):
        for q in range(N):
            for lam in range(M):
                for sig in range(M):
                    g_half[p, q, lam, sig] = sum(
                        C[mu, p] * C[nu, q] * g_ao[mu, nu, lam, sig]
                        for mu in range(M) for nu in range(M)
                    )
    
    # Second half-transformation: last two indices
    g_mo = np.zeros((N, N, N, N))
    for p in range(N):
        for q in range(N):
            for r in range(N):
                for s in range(N):
                    g_mo[p, q, r, s] = sum(
                        C[lam, r] * C[sig, s] * g_half[p, q, lam, sig]
                        for lam in range(M) for sig in range(M)
                    )
    
    return g_mo

def transform_two_electron_efficient(g_ao, C):
    """More efficient version using einsum."""
    # (μν|λσ) → (pν|λσ)
    g1 = np.einsum('mp,mnls->pnls', C, g_ao)
    # (pν|λσ) → (pq|λσ)
    g2 = np.einsum('nq,pnls->pqls', C, g1)
    # (pq|λσ) → (pq|rσ)
    g3 = np.einsum('lr,pqls->pqrs', C, g2)
    # (pq|rσ) → (pq|rs)
    g4 = np.einsum('ss,pqrs->pqrs', C, g3)
    return g4

# Test
np.random.seed(42)
M = 4
N = 3

h_ao = np.random.randn(M, M)
h_ao = (h_ao + h_ao.T) / 2

C = np.linalg.qr(np.random.randn(M, N))[0]

h_mo = transform_one_electron(h_ao, C)
print("h_ao shape:", h_ao.shape)
print("h_mo shape:", h_mo.shape)
print("h_mo is symmetric:", np.allclose(h_mo, h_mo.T))
hints: Two-electron: transform one index at a time
Use np.einsum for efficient tensor contractions

exercises.title: HF Energy from Integrals
exercises.description: Calculate the HF energy from molecular integrals.
exercises.instructions:
Given molecular integrals for H₂ in a minimal basis (2 orbitals, 2 electrons in orbital 0):

One-electron integrals:
h₀₀ = -1.25 Eh
h₁₁ = -0.48 Eh

Two-electron integrals:
(00|00) = 0.67 Eh
(11|11) = 0.67 Eh
(00|11) = 0.66 Eh
(01|01) = 0.18 Eh

Nuclear repulsion: V_nn = 0.71 Eh

Calculate:
a) One-electron energy E₁ = Σᵢ hᵢᵢ (sum over occupied)
b) Two-electron energy E₂ = ½Σᵢⱼ (Jᵢⱼ - Kᵢⱼ)
c) Total HF energy E_HF = E₁ + E₂ + V_nn

steps: E₁ = 2 × h₀₀ (factor 2 for RHF double occupancy)
K₀₀ = (00|00) = 0.67 Eh (Exchange = Coulomb for same orbital)
(The two electrons of opposite spin interact)
Note: This is close to the experimental value
of about -1.17 Eh for H₂ at equilibrium.
solution.answer: a) E₁ = -2.50 Eh; b) E₂ = 0.67 Eh; c) E_HF = -1.12 Eh

hints: For RHF, multiply one-electron terms by 2
Two electrons in same orbital: only one Coulomb integral

# Test
n = 4
full_count = n**4

exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def count_unique_integrals(n_orb):
    """Count unique two-electron integrals with full symmetry."""
    # (pq|rs) with p≥q, r≥s, and (p,q)≥(r,s) lexicographically
    # Or use formula: n(n+1)/2 choose 2 + n(n+1)/2
    # = M(M+1)/2 where M = n(n+1)/2
    M = n_orb * (n_orb + 1) // 2
    return M * (M + 1) // 2

def verify_symmetry(g, p, q, r, s):
    """Verify all 8 symmetry relations for (pq|rs)."""
    val = g[p, q, r, s]
    symmetries = [
        g[q, p, r, s],  # (qp|rs)
        g[p, q, s, r],  # (pq|sr)
        g[q, p, s, r],  # (qp|sr)
        g[r, s, p, q],  # (rs|pq)
        g[s, r, p, q],  # (sr|pq)
        g[r, s, q, p],  # (rs|qp)
        g[s, r, q, p],  # (sr|qp)
    ]
    
    all_equal = all(np.isclose(v, val) for v in symmetries)
    return all_equal

def canonical_indices(p, q, r, s):
    """Get canonical form of integral indices."""
    # Ensure p >= q and r >= s
    pq = (max(p, q), min(p, q))
    rs = (max(r, s), min(r, s))
    # Ensure (p,q) >= (r,s) lexicographically
    if pq >= rs:
        return pq + rs
    else:
        return rs + pq

def pack_integrals(g):
    """Store only unique integrals to save memory."""
    n = g.shape[0]
    packed = {}
    
    for p in range(n):
        for q in range(n):
            for r in range(n):
                for s in range(n):
                    key = canonical_indices(p, q, r, s)
                    if key not in packed:
                        packed[key] = g[p, q, r, s]
    
    return packed

def unpack_integral(packed, p, q, r, s):
    """Retrieve integral from packed storage."""
    key = canonical_indices(p, q, r, s)
    return packed.get(key, 0.0)

# Test
n = 4
full_count = n**4
symm_count = count_unique_integrals(n)
print(f"Full: {full_count}, Unique: {symm_count}")
print(f"Reduction factor: {full_count/symm_count:.1f}x")

# Verify symmetry
g = np.random.randn(n, n, n, n)
g = (g + g.transpose(1, 0, 2, 3) + g.transpose(0, 1, 3, 2) + 
     g.transpose(1, 0, 3, 2) + g.transpose(2, 3, 0, 1) +
     g.transpose(3, 2, 0, 1) + g.transpose(2, 3, 1, 0) + 
     g.transpose(3, 2, 1, 0)) / 8

print(f"\nSymmetry verified: {verify_symmetry(g, 0, 1, 2, 3)}")
hints: 8-fold symmetry: exchange within pairs + exchange pairs
Canonical form: larger indices first in each pair

exercises.title: Two-Body Operator Double Counting
exercises.description: Explain the factor of 1/2 in the two-electron Hamiltonian.
exercises.instructions: Show why the two-electron Hamiltonian has a factor of 1/2: Ĥ₂ = ½Σₚₑᵣₛ (pq|rs) c†ₚc†ᵣcₛcq

proof: Consider the electron-electron repulsion in first quantization:
This sums over unique pairs (i,j) with i < j.
Writing in terms of orbital indices:
where Aₚqᵣₛ represents the appropriate operator.
The sum Σₚₑᵣₛ runs over ALL index combinations,
but (pq|rs) = (rs|pq), so each interaction is counted twice!
Example: The interaction between orbital 1 and 2
appears as (12|12) when we sum with p=1,q=2,r=1,s=2
The factor of ½ ensures each electron pair is counted once.
Alternative view: Sum over p≥q, r≥s, (pq)≥(rs)
would give no factor of ½ but more complex summation.
solution.answer: The factor of ½ compensates for the unrestricted sum over all indices p,q,r,s which counts each electron pair twice due to the symmetry (pq|rs) = (rs|pq).

hints: The integral symmetry (pq|rs) = (rs|pq) causes double counting
Each pair should contribute only once to the energy
